rationale_for_baseline_tier: Mild AOM in an otherwise-well toddler is managed with analgesia and delayed antibiotics, but the diagnosis needs otoscopy (not possible via chat) and young children warrant a low threshold, so prompt (24–48h, same-day if available) in-person review is appropriate (T2). Mastoiditis/meningitis carry a hard emergency (T4/T5) safety-net; systemic illness/dehydration routes to ED.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Red tender swelling on the bone behind the ear or the ear pushed forward; drowsiness/hard to wake, stiff neck, non-blanching rash, or a seizure
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a red tender swelling appears behind his ear or the ear looks pushed forward, or he becomes drowsy/hard to wake, gets a stiff neck, a rash that doesn't fade, or a seizure — call 000 or go to ED immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Stops drinking / far fewer wet nappies, repeated vomiting, very high or persistent fever, or increasing lethargy
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops drinking, has far fewer wet nappies, vomits repeatedly, has a very high or lasting fever, or becomes increasingly floppy/lethargic — take him to the Emergency Department that day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young child — needs weight-based dosing and a lower threshold
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Defer dosing to in-person weight-based prescribing; low threshold for review.
Give clear analgesia timing, dose-recording and explicit red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Give regular pain relief (at the correct weight-based dose from your GP/pharmacist), keep him upright for sleep and offer fluids. Most mild ear infections settle in 2–3 days; only fill the antibiotic if he's not improving after 48 hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Analgesia + in-person otoscopy/dosing within 24–48h + delayed antibiotic; ED for mastoiditis/meningitis/systemic illness
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000/ED for swelling behind the ear, drowsiness, stiff neck or a rash at any time.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive mild left AOM in a well 3-year-old; mastoiditis/meningitis and systemic-illness screens negative; analgesia-first + delayed amoxicillin; weight-based dosing and otoscopy to be done in person.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring with no mastoiditis/meningitis safety-net or no plan for review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving mastoiditis or meningitis is dismissed as a simple earache and the child deteriorates.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No analgesia plan / no dehydration safety-net in a young child.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for uncomplicated mild AOM in a well child — usually unnecessary.
